name: Petrified Forest
description: The forest clearing you remember is... wrong. The trees stand rigid, their bark turned grey stone, branches frozen mid-sway like the arms of statues reaching for help that never came. The wildflowers are granite. The grass crunches underfoot -- not with the sound of frost, but of crumbling rock. The petrification curse has reached the outskirts, and it is not subtle about it. The air smells of dust and dying magic. Somewhere ahead, Castle Erelhain rises through the stone canopy, its towers wrapped in grey like a kingdom wearing its own funeral shroud.

responses.look: Trees turned to stone, every ring preserved in grey granite. The bark's texture is flawless -- you can count the knots, trace the grain. Nature's portrait, rendered in the medium of catastrophe. Some branches still have stone leaves. Others snapped under their own weight and lie in rocky fragments on the ground.
responses.use: You're not sure how one 'uses' a petrified tree. As a monument to failure? As a cautionary tale? As a very expensive garden ornament?
responses.take: You try to snap off a stone branch. It breaks, and a chunk of petrified wood falls to the ground. It's heavy, cold, and deeply sad. You leave it where it fell.
responses.talk: You address the stone trees. They do not respond. They have been silenced more permanently than any conversation partner you've encountered, and you once argued with a wall.
responses.default: You examine the petrified trees. They are a sobering reminder that time is not on your side. Or anyone's side. Time is on its own side.

responses.look: The wildflowers that once bloomed in defiant cheerfulness are now frozen in stone. Every petal, every stem, perfectly preserved and utterly lifeless. They were the first things you saw when you arrived in Erelhain. Now they're the first evidence of what you're trying to stop.
responses.take: You pick a stone flower. It snaps at the stem with a dry crack. The petals are cold and heavy in your palm. This is not a souvenir. This is a deadline.
responses.default: You regard the stone flowers. They are beautiful, in the way that endings are sometimes beautiful.

responses.look: The petrification advances in a visible line across the ground. Behind you: stone. Ahead: not yet stone, but getting there. The boundary creeps forward as you watch -- slow, but inexorable. Grass turns grey, then rigid, then cracks. The curse is patient, and it has no intention of stopping.
responses.use: You cannot 'use' an advancing magical curse. You can run from it, or you can stop it. Those are the options. There is no third option. Well, there's 'become a statue,' but that hardly counts.
responses.default: You watch the curse spread. It's hypnotic. It's also terrifying. You should probably stop watching and start doing something about it.

responses.look: Trees turned to grey stone, still reaching for a sky that can no longer nourish them. The bark texture is preserved perfectly, which somehow makes it sadder.
responses.take: They're rooted in stone ground. Everything here is stone. That's rather the point.
responses.use: You knock on one. It rings like a bell. Even the echoes sound mournful.
responses.default: You regard the stone trees. They stand as monuments to a forest that once was.

actions.text: The curse is accelerating. You can see it -- the grey line creeps forward faster than before, as if sensing that someone finally has the means to stop it and is determined to petrify the kingdom before they get the chance. The Crystal of Mundanity hums in your pack, responding to the magic. Time is running out. The Rite of Administrative Closure awaits in the throne room, but you'll need more than a crystal and good intentions. Much more.

petrify-touch.title: Death by Petrification
petrify-touch.narratorText: You press your bare hand against a petrified tree. The curse is patient, but it is also opportunistic. Grey spreads from the bark to your fingertips with the speed of gossip in a small village. Your hand goes rigid. Then your wrist. Then your arm. You watch yourself turn to stone with the detached fascination of someone who knows they've made a terrible mistake but can't look away from it. In your final moments, you become a very realistic statue. Pigeons will, eventually, find you.

dynamicDescriptions.curse_accelerating: The Petrified Forest stands in grey silence, the curse visibly advancing through the trees. The Crystal of Mundanity pulses in your inventory, resonating with the dark magic. The path to Castle Erelhain lies east, through stone trees and the ruins of a once-green kingdom.
